title: Separating Prompts from Application Logic
concept: Externalizing prompt text into config or a prompt registry instead of hardcoding strings.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: why did we say prompts need version control and rollback, just like code?

options.text: Because prompts can silently change or 'drift' over time, and a bad edit needs to be traceable and reversible
Because AI models refuse to run without a version number attached
Because version control makes prompts run faster on the GPU

exercises.explain: Right — prompts drift like code: small edits pile up, and without versioning you can't tell what changed or roll back a bad update. That's why treating prompts like code (with history and rollback) matters.

cards.title: Where Did That Prompt Go?
cards.body: A teammate needs to fix a typo in your AI's instructions. But the text is buried inside a function, 200 lines deep, in a file called utils_v2_final.py. Finding it takes 20 minutes — that's a hardcoding problem.

cards.title: What Does 'Separating Prompts' Mean?
cards.body: Instead of typing prompt text straight into your app's code (like 'prompt = "Summarize this..."' buried in a function), you store it somewhere else — a config file, database, or prompt registry. Your code then just asks for the prompt by name.

cards.title: The Recipe Card, Not the Chef's Memory
cards.body: A restaurant writes recipes on cards in a binder, not scribbled in the chef's head. Anyone can update the card without retraining the chef. A prompt registry is that recipe binder for your AI app.

cards.title: Hardcoded vs. Externalized
cards.body: Hardcoded: your Python file has the line 'system_prompt = "You are a helpful support bot..."' typed right in the middle of the code. Externalized: your code says 'get_prompt("support_bot_greeting")' and the actual wording lives in a prompts.yaml file or a database table anyone can edit.

cards.title: Myth: 'It's just a string, hardcoding is fine'
cards.body: A prompt isn't just a string — it's a piece of behavior that changes often, needs testing, and often gets edited by non-coders like prompt writers or product managers. Treating it like a random variable name hides it from the people who need to change it safely.

cards.title: Two Ways to Store a Prompt
cards.body: Same prompt, two very different setups.

left.text: Prompt text typed directly inside app.py, scattered across 10 files, mixed with logic

right.text: Prompt text lives in one prompts.yaml or registry, code just calls it by name

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the clearest sign that prompts are NOT separated from application logic?

options.text: Prompt text is typed directly inside function bodies across many files
Prompts are stored in a YAML file with names and version numbers
A non-engineer can edit a prompt without touching the codebase
Prompts are fetched from a database table by ID

exercises.explain: When prompt text is typed directly inside functions across files, it's mixed into the logic — that's hardcoding. The other options all describe prompts living outside the code, which is the separated approach.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Storing prompts in a separate config file means you can update wording without redeploying your entire application.
exercises.explain: True! If your code just fetches prompts by name from an external file or database, updating the file (or database entry) changes the behavior instantly — no code redeploy needed, which is a huge speed win.

exercises.sentence: Instead of hardcoding text, teams store prompts in a central registry so the code can just call get_prompt to fetch them.
exercises.explain: get_prompt represents the idea of asking the registry for the prompt by name, instead of the code containing the actual wording itself.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three descriptions of a 'prompt registry' sounds like AI-generated filler rather than a real, useful description?

options.text: A prompt registry is a centralized place — like a config file or database — where prompt text is stored with names and versions, separate from app code.
A prompt registry is a revolutionary paradigm-shifting solution that synergizes prompt excellence to unlock unprecedented AI-powered value across the enterprise.
A prompt registry lets engineers update wording without redeploying the whole application, since the code just looks up the prompt by name.

exercises.explain: Option B is full of vague buzzwords ('paradigm-shifting', 'synergizes', 'unprecedented value') but says nothing concrete about what a prompt registry actually does. The other two options describe real, specific mechanics.

exercises.prompt: Your team's app has 'summarize_prompt = "Summarize this in 3 bullet points..."' typed directly inside 15 different Python files across the codebase. What's the BEST fix, combining what you know about drift and separation?

options.text: Move the prompt text into a central prompt registry/config, and have all 15 files reference it by name and version
Copy-paste the exact same string into any new files too, to keep things consistent
Delete 14 of the copies and keep only one, chosen at random
Add a comment above each hardcoded prompt explaining what it does

exercises.explain: Centralizing the prompt solves both problems at once: it stops drift (one source of truth to version and roll back) and stops hardcoding (logic just references a name/version instead of owning the text). Comments or random deletion don't fix the root issue — the text is still scattered and un-versioned. This is the foundation you'll build on as we cover templating, testing, and deployment strategies next!
